data.title: SPX Call Demand Jumps On Optimism
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities gained across asset classes last week as the Iran war dragged into its fourth week. Oil volatility increased, with WTI 1M implied vol up 10 pts to 81% on the back of continued demand for upside calls as traders positioned for potential prolonged disruption to oil supply. Gold volatility jumped higher, with GLD 1M implied vol increasing over 9 pts to near a 5-year high of 41%. As we highlighted last week, positioning in gold options has shifted notably, with demand for puts picking up in recent weeks amidst the geopolitical turmoil. GLD 1M skew (25-delta ratio) has steepened to almost a 10-year high after being persistently inverted for most of the past two years. Rates volatility gained modestly as traders weighed the higher inflation backdrop with the hit to the growth outlook, while credit volatility continued its upward climb. VIXHY Index has more than tripled since the beginning of the year to a high of 320 bps vol – higher than the Q4 2018 growth scare (when SPX Index sold off 20%).

Equity Volatility: The VIX Index jumped 4.3 pts to 31% last week, ending the week at its highest level since last year’s April sell-off. Notably, however, there’s been very little panic in the options market. Even when SPX Index fell 3.4% on Thu/Fri, the bid to volatility came more from the upside than the downside as investors positioned for a potential rebound (aka “the TACO Trade”). In fact, put skew and put convexity both declined as investors used the pullback to monetize existing hedges. SPX 1M put skew (25-delta vs. 50-delta ratio) has now fallen to near a 1-year low (2nd percentile over the past year). Instead, we saw demand for upside calls pick up going into the weekend, adding almost 2 pts to the 5.7 pt increase in the VIX Index on Thu/Fri. See Exhibit 2. Will the “TACO Trade” work this time? The optimism in the equity options market contrasts with the caution we’re seeing in the cross-asset vol market, with the oil market signaling potentially an extended period of supply disruption and the credit market starting to price in a more severe growth slowdown. Within equities, EM countries are bearing the brunt of the energy shock, with EEM 1M implied volatility nearing its “Liberation Day” high of 42% already (vs. the VIX Index which is still 20+ pts below its Apr’25 peak). The EEM-SPX 1M implied volatility spread widened to a 10-year high of 16% last week (vs. avg spread of 3.7%).

Correlation: Stocks are becoming more correlated as macro headlines overshadow fundamentals, with the COR1M Index gaining another 3 pts last week to 42%. More concerning is the decline in equity-rates correlation, which has flipped to negative as inflation worries have hit both stocks and bonds simultaneously. The 1-month rolling correlation between SPX Index and the 10Y Tsy yield has dropped from +72% to -53% (see Exhibit 3).

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e. rich) while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e. cheap)

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g. lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using zscores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross-asset volatility markets

Cross-Asset Correlation Analysis

Macro Equity Volatility

VIX Index Volatility

US Index Volatility

Disclaimers

There are important risks associated with transacting in any of the Cboe Company products discussed here. Before engaging in any transactions in those products, it is important for market participants to carefully review the disclosures and disclaimers contained at: Disclosures and Disclaimers Related to Cboe Products and Services | Cboe.

These products are complex and are suitable only for sophisticated market participants. In certain jurisdictions, Cboe Company products are only permitted for investment professionals, certified sophisticated investors, or high net worth corporations and associations. These products involve the risk of loss, which can be substantial and, depending on the type of product, can exceed the amount of money deposited in establishing the position. Market participants should put at risk only funds that they can afford to lose without affecting their lifestyle.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CCD: Convertibles Can Leverage Equity Exposure, Time To Buy The Dip
data.description:
The Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund (CCD) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with income through exposure to convertible and corporate bonds, diversified across multiple sectors through strategic asset allocation. The strategy has the ability to provide investors with equity-like exposure as part of their fixed income allocation, potentially geared towards risk-taking investors seeking differentiated returns. With global supply chains being disrupted by the Iranian war, the equity markets have faced significant pressure, resulting in negative pricing action for CCD. Despite the near-term headwinds, I believe investors may opportunistically build a position in CCD with the expectation of a market turnaround as events in the Middle East are resolved; I am recommending CCD with a Buy rating. Before purchasing shares, investors should consider the risks involved in holding CCD, as the strategy’s performance may be significantly influenced by further equity price decline.

Investment Thesis for CCD

Fundamentally, convertibles will inherently trade similarly to the equity markets given the underlying structure of the bonds. Convertible bonds are generally linked to the value of the underlying shares based on the conversion rate, or the rate at which the bond will convert into shares at the time of maturity. As a result of this, these bond issuances may generally trade similarly to the equity markets, presenting investors with differentiated performance with respect to investment-grade and plain vanilla issuances.

While this can be an appealing feature during periods of positive pricing action in the equity markets, the bonds may face similar directional change when the equity markets sell off, as exhibited in the total return chart below. Comparing 6-month performance, CCD has far outpaced the S&P 500 (SPX), though it has recently experienced a similar sell-off in recent weeks, likely the result of the global supply chain disruption with the Iranian war taking shape.

This could become a challenging market environment for CCD going forward if the war remains ongoing and intensifies despite signs of talks between the US and Iran.

I believe the greatest pressure on the domestic market is heightened oil prices, which may add significant uncertainty to the forward rate of inflation that may hinder economic development for 2026. The rationale is that higher oil prices may pressure consumers in relation to their discretionary spending budgets, potentially imposing greater risk to the consumer-focused market. In addition to this, higher oil prices may impact the industrial sector, specifically transportation, petrochemicals, and other markets that rely on cheap oil as an input.

In addition to heightened oil prices, other commodities may impact the global market and, in turn, impact the domestic market environment. While domestic natural gas prices have been tame since the start of the war, international benchmarks have experienced substantial increases in gas prices, which may disrupt global productivity with higher electricity costs.

Another resource that may face supply constraints due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is helium, a critical gas used in photolithography used for the printing of semiconductor circuits. Some of the top holdings within CCD include convertible bonds issued by Western Digital (WDC), Lumentum (LITE), MSK Inc. (MKSI), and Seagate (STX), which may be exposed to the helium shortage.

Overall, companies’ issuances held in CCD may be exposed to the global supply chain risks resulting from the Iranian war, potentially negatively impacting performance in the near term. While I don’t expect this war to be ongoing, there’s no telling just how long the Strait of Hormuz will be shut to the global economy. With rippling impacts on international trade and the supply of resources, I suspect inflationary pressures in the US may rise over the coming months, potentially negatively impacting the equity markets as well as the convertibles held in CCD.

That being said, timing the end of the war isn’t my area of expertise, and as an investor, we should be opportunistic when others are fearful. The fact of the matter is that CCD has declined by nearly -9.5% from its recent high in February 2026, potentially presenting an opportunity for investors seeking to build a position at a relatively lower price point. While CCD could continue to trend lower, particularly when considering the technical signals presented in the Ripster Cloud, averaging into a position over time may be an ideal opportunity as the fund declines.

Structurally, I don’t believe the impact from the war will negatively affect the quality of the bond issuances. On the other hand, because the convertible bonds are priced with respect to the share conversion rate, continued pressure in the equity markets will, in turn, affect the pricing of the convertible bond issuances.

The bottom line is that I believe investors may consider adding to their positions in CCD as the price faces pressure in anticipation of the market turning around. This will likely be an event-driven strategy, primarily associated with the war in Iran and the supply chain disruption tied to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Any signs of an end to the war or alleviation in the supply chain may positively influence the equity markets and CCD.

About Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund

CCD was launched by Calamos on March 27, 2015, on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has a total expense ratio of 237bps, inclusive of a 100bps management fee and 131bps leverage fee. CCD currently has $906mm in managed assets ($621mm in share assets) when applying the 31.43% leverage rate with an average daily trading volume of 84k shares.

The strategy pays out a robust monthly distribution annualized at $2.34/share over the last twelve months, yielding 11.35%.

Risks Related to CCD

CCD is a managed closed-end fund designed to invest across convertible and high-yield corporate bonds, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Issuances held within the strategy may provide a similar risk profile and trade with respect to the equity markets, potentially exposing investors to greater price volatility relative to investment-grade corporate bonds. CCD is actively managed, exposing investors to manager risk and their ability to appropriately select and weight securities. Global supply chain risks may result in continued challenges to the equity markets and negatively influence the price performance of CCD. Convertible issuances may be particularly sensitive to the share price of the underlying securities as a result of their respective conversion rates, potentially impacting the value of the bonds if the equity market were to significantly decline.

Final Thoughts

Investing in CCD can be an opportunistic investment strategy following the significant price decline in recent weeks. With the equity markets facing greater volatility resulting from heightened oil prices following the military mobilization in Iran, shares may face continued pressure until events in the region are resolved. Following the price decline in CCD, I believe investors may opportunistically buy CCD or average into a position with the expectation of a market turnaround in the future; I am recommending CCD with a Buy rating.

data.title: Tesla May Beat Deliveries, But This Could Hurt The Bottom Line
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) will soon release its Q1 2026 production and deliveries, and I expect the company to beat analyst estimates due to one key reason that I discuss in this article. However, for the same reason, I expect the company's profit margins to miss analyst estimates in Q1 2026 and remain depressed in the coming periods.

Previous Coverage

I most recently covered the company in my article Tesla: Challenging 2026 Ahead, in which I discussed Tesla's intensifying global competition, weakening demand, and persistent delays in autonomy and robotics, and assigned a Strong Sell rating. Tesla has declined by 20 percent since my article.

Q1 2026 Forecast

I expect Tesla to deliver more than 370,000 vehicles in Q1 2026, somewhat above the analyst consensus of 365,645. The following table shared by Tesla shows the average, median, and standard deviation of 23 analyst estimates:

The standard deviation of 25,941 indicates that analyst estimates have a wide range. In a normal distribution, approximately 68% of the data falls within one standard deviation, meaning 16 estimates likely were between 337,000 and 389,000, and seven estimates were outside of that range. This is a surprisingly wide range of estimates for a mega-cap company followed around the world by dozens, if not hundreds, of professional analysts as well as countless retail investors. This is important because, if the current quarter's estimates show such high uncertainty, the estimates for future periods may not be reliable.

I believe that the following topic helped Tesla's deliveries but will materially and adversely impact Tesla's profitability in Q1 2026 (figures expected April 22nd) and future periods.

0% APR and Subprime Financing

Ever since interest rates surged in 2022, Tesla has had to increasingly rely on price cuts and subvented (subsidized) interest rate financing to keep demand from falling sharply, but this comes at a cost to Tesla's profit margins and its bottom line.

In January 2023, Tesla slashed worldwide prices for its most popular vehicles by up to 20 percent, and Tesla continued to lower prices in all major markets throughout 2023 and 2024 in a series of price cuts. In 2025, Tesla expanded subvented interest rates and even interest-free financing offers in China, the U.S., and Europe. In October 2025, Tesla rolled out cheaper "Standard" Model 3 and Model Y trims, about $5,000 below the previous base models, following the expiration of the generous EV tax credits in the United States.

Tesla Is Financing Subprime Credit

I have not seen this topic widely discussed, but I believe it is one of the costliest steps that Tesla had to take in recent years to shore up demand.

Historically, Tesla limited subvented interest-rate financing offers to "well-qualified buyers with excellent credit" profiles, but in Q1, I noticed that Tesla began offering a 2.99 percent financing rate to buyers with credit scores less than 640, or "Fair" credit profiles, for loan terms of up to 72 months.

If the buyer selects a Term greater than 72 months, namely 84 months, then the rate jumps to 11.67 percent, which I believe is the market rate for buyers with Fair credit profiles, as the following screenshot shows:

Tesla appears to be moving further down the credit spectrum by subsidizing the interest rate for buyers with Fair credit, lowering the monthly payments, in order to keep its deliveries from declining at a faster rate. This dynamic likely requires Tesla to make an upfront payment, the present value of the difference in monthly payments over the term of the loan, to third-party financiers to be able to offer subsidized rates to potential buyers.

This practice, however, comes at a cost. Given the difference between the subvented and the market interest rates, I estimate that the one-time payment from Tesla to the third-party financier could be as high as $10,000 for buyers with Fair credit profiles, financing a $45,000 loan for 72 months. This amount declines as the size of the down-payment increases.

I believe that Tesla books this expense to Cost of Sales with each delivery, so I expect this practice to pressure Tesla Automotive's profit margins in 2026. Depending on the prevalence of buyers with Fair credit in Tesla's buyer pool, which only Tesla knows, this dynamic could compress Tesla's automotive gross margins and company-wide operating margin by multiple percentage points.

As a sensitivity analysis, if buyers with Fair credit account for 5 percent, 10 percent, or 15 percent of Tesla's financed deliveries, and the size of Tesla's upfront payment to the third-party financier is in the several thousands of dollars range, then the downward pressure on Tesla's automotive gross margin would be 50-100 basis points in the low-adoption case, 100-200 basis points in the medium-adoption case, and 200-300 basis points in the high-adoption case, respectively.

Risks To My Bear Thesis

First, even though Tesla is offering subvented rates to buyers with Fair credit, it is not advertising the practice, so the prevalence may be low, limiting the downward pressure on Tesla's Automotive gross margin.

Second, if automotive loan interest rates decline overall, then the amount of the one-time payment from Tesla to third-party financiers would decline.

Third, Tesla's profitability in other departments, namely Energy and Services, or a material increase in FSD subscriptions, could partly offset the downward pressure from price cuts and subvented interest-rate loans.

Bottom Line

Tesla is dipping deeper into the credit spectrum to generate demand for its vehicles in the face of intensifying competition, but subsidizing fair-credit buyers is costly and not discussed widely across the investment community.

I expect Tesla to beat analyst consensus for Q1 2026 deliveries, but in the following months, the reality of low profit margins and possible net losses may become front and center. I maintain my $55 per share price target and Strong Sell rating.

data.title: Argan Surges 38% Post-Earnings: 3 Best Stocks With Similar Growth Potential
data.description:
Industry Drivers Support Construction and Engineering Growth

Alpha Picks stock winner, Argan, Inc. (AGX), surged 38% Friday, reminding investors that not all stocks are impacted by the same macro pressures. While markets remain focused on higher oil prices and inflation arising from geopolitical uncertainty, AGX delivered a blowout earnings report highlighted by a $2.9 billion project backlog, which provides clear earnings visibility into demand for large-scale power infrastructure.

This type of sector- and industry-specific strength reinforces an important theme in today’s market. Select segments of the economy, particularly within Industrials, are benefiting from large-scale infrastructure investment trends that are not directly impacted by short-term macro volatility. In industries like Construction and Engineering, multi-year project backlogs can translate into earnings strength and continued price momentum.

In this article, I highlight three Quant Strong Buys in the Construction and Engineering industry that are well-positioned to capitalize on these demand trends and potentially follow AGX’s lead.

How I Selected the Best Industrials Stocks to Buy Now

To select the best stocks to follow AGX’s lead, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and targeted Quant Strong Buys. Then I selected the sector & industry filter so I could sort for targeted stocks in the Industrials sector and the Construction and Engineering industry. I dug further to find compelling backlog stories that reveal strong earnings visibility.

1. Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.89B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 7 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 1 out of 37

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

One of my Alpha Picks holdings, Sterling Infrastructure, is a market leader in large-scale E-infrastructure solutions like data centers, as well as transportation and manufacturing facilities. What sets STRL apart from many of its competitors is its increasing focus on higher-margin growth markets tied to AI, reshoring, and digital infrastructure. Like AGX, Sterling’s strong backlog growth and sector-leading margins signal potential for earnings growth in 2026. This leads us to STRL’s Quant Factor strengths in Profitability and Momentum.

Sterling’s 19.49% Return on Total Capital, which is 179% ahead of sector peers, demonstrates its ability to invest its capital effectively into high-margin projects. Meanwhile, Sterling’s sector-leading Cash From Operations provides the financial flexibility needed to reinvest in new growth opportunities. In their recent quarterly earnings report, Joseph Cutillo, CEO, remarked on how the company achieved "strong revenue growth of over 32% and adjusted diluted EPS growth of over 53%." He also highlighted, "Full year gross margins reached 23%, and adjusted EBITDA margins exceeded 20% for the first time in our history." Cutillo also noted that 2025 was the fifth consecutive year of over 35% adjusted EPS growth. This profitability and growth story helps to support the company’s 'A+' price momentum.

STRL crushes its Industrials sector peers so far in 2026 with its three-month performance of 32.76% compared to -3.49% for the sector median. Price momentum is a Quant Factor because it is predictive of future performance, especially when combined with other factors, such as Growth and Profitability. This is in part attributable to strong investor demand and positive sentiment, which can drive further buying and price appreciation in the short term, especially when earnings support the growth stories like AGX and STRL. When momentum is supported by strong fundamentals and positive industry trends, STRL stands out as a sector leader, which sets the stage for another high-quality operator in the Construction and Engineering industry.

2. MasTec, Inc. (MTZ)

  • Market Capitalization: $24.60B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 25 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 3 out of 37

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

MasTec, Inc. is a leading U.S.-based infrastructure company focused on energy, utility, and communications projects, including renewable energy and oil and gas pipeline construction. Its exposure extends to data center-related work, which opens further growth opportunities. With a growing backlog, MTZ is well-positioned to expand profitability and maintain its earnings visibility into 2026, which is demonstrated by its ‘A-’ Growth Factor grade.

MTZ’s outstanding growth metrics and impressive backlog were highlighted by CEO Jose Mas in their February earnings call. Mas stated:

In summary, we exceeded guidance again in revenue, EBITDA and EPS, highlighting another strong execution quarter and year for MasTec. This strong result is in part a testament to the scale and diversification MasTec has achieved over time, and we are excited about our outlook for 2026 and beyond given the clear long-term positive market conditions across all of the end markets we serve.

The company’s forward EBITDA Growth is 19.27%, which is more than double the sector median, while its forward long-term earnings growth (3-5Y CAGR) of 31% is over 150% higher than sector peers. Mas supported its forward growth, stating, "On a full year basis, backlog was up over $4.5 billion, a 33% annual increase. Sequentially, backlog was up over $2 billion, representing a 1.6x book-to-bill." Continuation of this growth can support the company’s outstanding momentum.

At over 40% appreciation for the past three months, MTZ supports its 'A+' momentum grade, putting in greater than 40% performance over the past three months and about 166% for the past year. Continued expansion in clean energy and infrastructure construction can also create long-term demand in the short term as well as the long run, leading into our third look at Industrials sector leaders.

3. MYR Group Inc. (MYRG)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.25B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 14 out of 611

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/30/2026): 2 out of 37

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

A construction and engineering industry leader for more than 130 years, MYR Group provides large-scale electrical construction services for utilities and commercial customers, including data centers. This gives MYRG a strong competitive advantage, as backlog strength is supported by rising power demand. The company’s exposure to renewable energy diversifies its long-term growth potential. From a Quant perspective, MYRG’s Growth and Revisions scorecards point to more momentum potential.

MYRG’s forward EBITDA Growth of over 38% is more than four times the sector median’s measure of just above 8%, while the company’s sector-crushing forward Operating Cash Flow of 46.65% stands to help fund its pursuit of new opportunities. At its recent quarterly earnings call, President and CEO Richard Swartz highlighted a "steady backlog of $2.8 billion at the end of 2025," driven by a "healthy bidding environment and the continued investment in infrastructure to meet the growing electrification needs across the U.S. and Canada." Like AGX and our featured Construction and Engineering stocks, this backstory can support a continuation of the company’s stellar price momentum.

MYRG’s 136% one-year price performance looks to extend to another year of sector-leading momentum as the past three months’ gains surpass 21%. The company’s disciplined operations, strong backlog, and positioning within critical infrastructure markets provide potential for further momentum in 2026, bringing us to the broader conclusion on these Quant Strong Buy stocks.

Conclusion: Industrials Sector Maintains Growth Potential

AGX’s 38% surge following Friday’s earnings highlights a broader theme, which is the potential for Industrials, particularly select Construction and Engineering companies, to benefit from cyclical demand. Furthermore, while the geopolitical and macro environment weighs on market sentiment, high-quality companies like STRL, MTZ, and MYRG with strong backlogs can attract investor attention and continue pre-war price momentum. In a market dominated by uncertainty and volatility, these Quant Strong Buys offer a compelling case for maintaining exposure to high-quality industrial growth stocks.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives or consider PRO Quant Portfolio, which we launched in June. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top 'Strong Buy' quant stocks. Feel free to check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing. Happy Investing!

data.title: Yesway Restarts IPO For Debt Reduction And Continued Expansion
data.description:
Yesway Is Growing Profitably

Yesway, Inc. (YSWY) has filed to raise public capital from an IPO of its Class A common stock, per an S-1 registration statement recently filed with the SEC.

The company operates a network of convenience stores mostly in lower-tier and rural markets in the central and southern United States.

Revenue is growing moderately, but the firm is profitable and is pursuing both organic growth (new store development) and acquisition growth drivers to expand its footprint.

The IPO will likely be in demand from institutional investors, assuming management’s valuation assumptions are reasonable.

What Does Yesway Do?

Yesway is expanding its network of convenience stores and fueling stations operating via its brands Yesway and Allsup’s.

The firm previously began an IPO process in late 2021, as I wrote about here, but ended that process due to unfavorable IPO market conditions at the time.

Since then, Yesway has grown its network by a net of 16 stores, while total revenue has risen from a run rate of about $1.6 billion in 2021 to $2.7 billion in 2025, and total liabilities have also risen materially.

Operating margin has also increased since the original IPO attempt, as has operating cash flow.

The company had 419 stores in seven states as of December 31, 2025, per the map below:

Yesway sells both third-party convenience store merchandise and its house brand products.

The firm was founded in 2015 by private equity firm Brookwood and has grown substantially through 27 acquisitions while building organically (90 stores by the end of 2025).

However, if Yesway wants to expand its geographic footprint, it would have logistics constraints in that it would have to build (or buy) distribution and other capabilities to support the expanded footprint while building brand density for marketing and logistics efficiencies of scale.

The company's 2025 revenue contribution by segment is shown in the pie chart below:

For convenience store operators with fuel operations, the fuel is typically a low-margin business designed to bring in traffic, whereas the inside merchandise sales are the relatively high-margin part of the business.

In Yesway’s example, the inside merchandise is producing the majority of the gross profit, with the 2025 gross margin at 35.2%.

Compared to competitors Murphy USA and Casey’s, Yesway lands in the middle of the two in terms of gross margin, with Casey’s at around 41% and Murphy at 31%, according to recent reports.

Yesway’s co-founder, Chairman, President, and CEO is Mr. Thomas N. Trkla, who is also Chairman and CEO of Brookwood, which has invested a fair market value investment of $823 million to date.

What is Yesway’s Market?

The company operates in the convenience store and refueling market in North America, which was estimated at approximately $1 trillion in size in 2021.

The global convenience store market was expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.6% from 2022 to 2028.

By region, the Asia Pacific region is expected to grow the fastest, at a CAGR of 6.4% during the forecast period.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory of the U.S. convenience store market through 2026, by product type:

The market is expected to continue its growth path as consumers seek ready-made meals or snacks while fueling their automobiles.

By segment, the foodservice segment is expected to produce the highest CAGR through 2028, at 6.4%.

The convenience store market is highly fragmented, with greater than 50% of stores operated by single-unit owners, but the list below includes various major players:

  • Casey's General Stores, Inc. (CASY).

  • Alimentation Couche-Tard Inc. (ANCTF).

  • Murphy USA Inc. (MUSA).

  • Parkland Corporation.

  • 7-Eleven.

  • FamilyMart.

  • ARKO Petroleum Corp. (APC).

  • Major fuel companies.

Recent industry trends include the continued consolidation by larger operators, an increased focus on foodservice as a driver of traffic, and only gradual technology adoption.

The company's strengths include its geographic focus, positioning in rural & suburban markets with reduced competition, and differentiated foodservice offerings in Allsup’s.

Weaknesses include its reliance on fuel-oriented traffic, lower-density rural market dynamics, and operational complexity with its multi-format stores.

Opportunities include new store development along with M&A potential, foodservice expansion, and private-label growth and optimization via improved technology and data.

Threats are electric vehicle adoption, regulatory changes, commodity price volatility, and competitors also seeking to acquire existing operators in local regions.

Yesway’s Recent Financial Results

The company's financial results show moderate top-line revenue growth, higher gross margin, increasing net margin, and improved cash flow from operations, as the table shows here:
